note: Nipissing University 'Banned & Challenged Books' research guide surfaced these titles; each entry below was independently verified against an authoritative primary source (mostly Freedom to Read Canada / freedomtoread.ca). Two libguide titles excluded after research: 'This Book Won't Burn' (no documented challenge) and 'The Book of Negroes' (only a symbolic street protest, not an institutional ban).

entries.title: Alligators All Around
entries.author: Maurice Sendak
entries.description: Challenged at a British Columbia public library on grounds of racism: the letter-I page depicts alligators 'imitating Indians', caricaturing Indigenous peoples. The book was retained.

entries.title: Asha's Mums
entries.author: Rosamund Elwin and Michele Paulse
entries.description: In 1997 Surrey, B.C. school trustees banned the use of children's books depicting same-sex parents in elementary grades; Asha's Mums was one of three titles. In Chamberlain v. Surrey School District No. 36 (2002) the Supreme Court of Canada ruled the ban unreasonable and contrary to B.C.'s secular School Act.

entries.title: Black Like Kyra, White Like Me
entries.author: Judith Vigna
entries.description: In 2000 a Toronto Public Library patron challenged the book, objecting that it 'reinforces negative stereotypes about blacks and positive types about whites.' The library retained it.

entries.title: A Hatful of Seuss
entries.author: Dr. Seuss
entries.description: In 2015 a British Columbia public-library patron objected to ethnic stereotyping in the included story 'If I Ran the Zoo' — 'orientalism, major Asian stereotyping in images and words' and 'exaggerated black-face characters' — and asked that it be removed. The library kept the book.

entries.title: The Headless Ghost
entries.author: R. L. Stine
entries.description: In 1995 a parent group asked the Halifax School Board to withdraw R. L. Stine's Goosebumps and Fear Street series (including this title), saying the books convey violence and a lack of respect for parental authority. The board let parents govern only their own children's reading; the books remained.

entries.title: On the Banks of Plum Creek
entries.author: Laura Ingalls Wilder
entries.description: In 1997 two parents in the Fort Garry School Division (Winnipeg, Manitoba) complained the book contained references offensive to Indigenous peoples. A review committee was convened but the complaint was withdrawn; the book was retained.

entries.title: Pride: Celebrating Diversity & Community
entries.author: Robin Stevenson
entries.description: Shortly after the 2016 publication, a Quebec teacher asked the author not to discuss the book during a grade 5-6 class visit, saying students hadn't yet learned about LGBTQ+ identities. The school allowed the visit after a response was drafted with the Canadian Children's Book Centre.

entries.title: Pride Puppy!
entries.author: Robin Stevenson
entries.description: Pride Puppy! was added to Montgomery County (Maryland) Public Schools' language-arts curriculum, then named in the 2023 lawsuit Mahmoud v. Taylor after the board removed religious opt-outs. The U.S. Supreme Court ruled 6-3 (June 2025) that the no-opt-out policy burdened parents' free-exercise rights; the title was removed from the approved curriculum in 2024 but stayed in school libraries.

entries.title: Salma Writes a Book
entries.author: Danny Ramadan
entries.description: In early 2024 the Waterloo Catholic District School Board 'shadow banned' the book — whose secondary storyline involves a gay uncle — keeping it off open shelves and requiring a request plus 'a Catholic understanding' first. After public outcry and an authors' open letter, the restriction was lifted.

entries.title: Sleeping Dragons All Around
entries.author: Sheree Fitch
entries.description: A library patron challenged the picture book, arguing it perpetuated negative stereotypes of Chinese people, language and culture. The book was retained. (Recorded in the CFLA / Freedom to Read challenges survey, reported November 2014.)

entries.title: Three Wishes: Palestinian and Israeli Children Speak
entries.author: Deborah Ellis
entries.description: In 2006 the Canadian Jewish Congress urged Ontario school boards to restrict elementary access to this non-fiction book on the Israeli-Palestinian conflict. Several boards (Toronto, Greater Essex, Ottawa-Carleton) limited it to Grade 7+, and York withdrew it from the Silver Birch reading program.

entries.title: The Waiting Dog
entries.author: Carolyn Beck
entries.description: In 2006 a parent complained to the Burlington Public Library (Ontario) about the picture book's graphic depictions of violence, calling it age-inappropriate. The library retained it, noting it met selection standards.

entries.title: 7 Generations: A Plains Cree Saga
entries.author: David A. Robertson
entries.description: In fall 2018 Edmonton Public Schools placed Robertson's Indigenous graphic-novel series on a 'not recommended' list, citing sensitive residential-school abuse content requiring guided discussion. The board removed the list after public criticism.

entries.title: Dance Me Outside
entries.author: W. P. Kinsella
entries.institution: Jean Vanier Roman Catholic school, Barrie
entries.description: In 1994 an Indigenous anti-racism alliance challenged the short-story collection at a Barrie, Ontario Roman Catholic school, arguing younger readers might miss Kinsella's irony. The local public library and schools declined to remove his books.

entries.title: The Graveyard Book Graphic Novel: Volume 1
entries.author: Neil Gaiman and P. Craig Russell
entries.description: The children's graphic-novel adaptation of The Graveyard Book (illustrated by P. Craig Russell) was challenged in Canada in 2015 for violence.

entries.title: The Great Bear
entries.author: David A. Robertson
entries.description: In April 2022 the Durham District School Board briefly pulled the second Misewa Saga book from shelves, saying Indigenous families had flagged potentially harmful stereotypes/terminology. After an accelerated community review the book was reinstated the same month.

entries.title: Hold Fast
entries.author: Kevin Major
entries.description: Kevin Major's 1978 Newfoundland YA novel was challenged in 1988-89 at an Estevan, Saskatchewan high-school library over coarse language and content; the book was kept. It was objected to repeatedly elsewhere over explicit language, perceived immorality and sexual content.

entries.title: Suffer Little Children
entries.author: Derek O'Brien
entries.description: The Newfoundland and Labrador government argued O'Brien's 1991 abuse memoir should not be sold during the Mount Cashel orphanage abuse trials, on prejudicial-publicity grounds. The sale restriction was later lifted; the book is now available in the province.

entries.title: The Apprenticeship of Duddy Kravitz
entries.author: Mordecai Richler
entries.description: In 1990 parents in Essex County, Ontario demanded the novel be removed from high-school reading lists over vulgarity and sexual content. The board kept the book but advised teachers to avoid undue controversy. Defended publicly by June Callwood and Al Purdy.

entries.title: The Collected Essex County
entries.author: Jeff Lemire
entries.description: In 2011 a library patron in Ontario challenged the graphic novel over offensive language. The specific library and resolution are not documented.

entries.title: The Godfather
entries.author: Mario Puzo
entries.description: In 2025 The Godfather appeared on Edmonton Public Schools' list of 226 titles slated for removal under an Alberta provincial order targeting sexually explicit material. A revised September 2025 order narrowed the rule to explicit visual images, which would likely spare a text-only novel.

entries.title: Lives of Girls and Women
entries.author: Alice Munro
entries.description: In 1976 a high-school principal in Peterborough, Ontario removed the novel from a Grade 13 reading list over explicit language and sex scenes. The case helped prompt the creation of Canada's Freedom to Read Week.

entries.title: The Wars
entries.author: Timothy Findley
entries.description: In 2011 a parent challenged the Grade 12 English novel at Ontario's Bluewater District School Board over depictions of sex and violence, including a brothel scene and a homosexual gang-rape scene. A review committee recommended it stay in the curriculum.
